root_motivation: Provide a Rust crate of small neural-network layer primitives (Linear, Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU, Softmax, LSTM, plus argmax) suitable for inference with const-array weights baked into source. The library is the foundation downstream tools (cradle, episode redirect detector, self-review classifier) will consume; without it those depend on candle/onnx and drag in 200MB+ model files. v0.1 ships layer primitives only — the bake CLI, audio frontends, and worked cat-RNN example are explicit follow-ons.
user_persona: A Rust author shipping a single binary who has a trained tiny model (<5MB of weights) and wants to call it as a pure function with no IO, no allocator, no runtime, and no extra binary asset. Concretely: me, writing wintermute tooling that wants a 50-neuron model embedded in an 8MB CLI.

acceptance_criteria.description: Linear (dense) layer: given a weight matrix W ([out, in]) and bias b ([out]), compute y = W * x + b for an input slice x ([in]). Output matches a hand-computed numeric reference within 1e-6.

Activation functions: sigmoid, tanh, relu, softmax. Each applied element-wise to a slice (softmax across the slice). Outputs match a numeric reference within 1e-6 for sigmoid/tanh/relu and 1e-6 for softmax.

Single-layer LSTM cell stepping: given weights (W_ih, W_hh, b) plus initial (h, c), step one timestep with input x and return the new (h, c). The step matches the canonical PyTorch LSTMCell formula (i,f,g,o gates split from a single Wx+Wh+b sum) within 1e-5.
LSTM scan: given a sequence of input frames and the LSTM weights, produce the final hidden state by stepping the cell across the frames in order. Output matches an iterative reference within 1e-5.
Argmax: given a slice of f32 logits, return the index of the maximum (first index on ties). Output matches naive iteration on a randomized property test (1000 cases).

End-to-end worked example (XOR-LSTM in tests): hand-set weights in a test file produce a tiny LSTM that classifies a 4-symbol sequence pattern; the test runs Lstm + Linear + Softmax + Argmax through the library and asserts the predicted class is correct for at least 3 of 4 canonical inputs. This exercises composition of all primitives, not a single primitive in isolation.

Allocation-free inference: a benchmark/integration test calls a representative composed pipeline (Lstm step + Linear + Sigmoid) using only caller-provided output buffers; no Vec / Box / heap allocation occurs inside the primitives' hot path. Verified by structuring the primitive APIs to write into `&mut [f32]` outputs rather than returning Vec; the test is a compile-time guarantee plus a runtime assertion that the same output buffer is reused across 100 calls without panic.
Determinism: running the same composed pipeline on the same inputs twice produces bit-identical f32 outputs.
Documented module rustdoc: every public item in the primitives module carries a doc comment with at least one runnable example or a math formula.

Domain-level invariants hold under randomized inputs: sigmoid stays in [0,1] and is monotonic, tanh stays in [-1,1], relu clamps at zero, softmax is a probability distribution, argmax is order-preserving under softmax, and argmax always returns a valid index for nonempty input. Verified via tests/acceptance_ac10.rs which delegates to proptest_invariants for property-based testing (7+ distinct properties, default 256 cases each).

scope: Element-wise activations: sigmoid, tanh, relu, softmax
LSTM cell step and LSTM scan over a sequence
f32-only numeric path (per PRD §6 non-goals: no GPU, no quantization research)
Pure Rust, scalar math, no SIMD intrinsics in v0.1
non_goals: Quantization (int8, q8, GPTQ, AWQ) — deferred per PRD §6.5
The bake CLI (`morsel bake`) that converts safetensors to weights.rs — deferred to a follow-on PRD per PRD §7 phasing (this is phase 0, that's phase 1)
Audio preprocessing (LogMel, Mfcc) — deferred per PRD §7 phase 3
The cat_meow worked example end-to-end with real ESC-50 weights — deferred per PRD §7 phase 2
Conv1d, Embedding, Gru — deferred per PRD §7 phase 3
SIMD acceleration — deferred per PRD §7 phase 4
no_std support — not promised in v0.1 (PRD §9.3 says 'day one if cheap', but core libm is not in std::f32::exp by default; defer to follow-on)

additional.rationale: max_deps=1 because primitives are pure scalar f32 math; the only allowed dependency is proptest (dev-dependency, not counted). Runtime deps target zero.

five_whys_trace.q: Why do you want a Rust crate of tiny neural-network primitives?
five_whys_trace.a: Because every wintermute tool that wants a small ML decision today either pulls in candle (huge), uses regex (brittle), or asks an LLM (slow and expensive). The middle is empty.

five_whys_trace.q: Why does the middle being empty actually matter to you right now?
five_whys_trace.a: Because cradle (a downstream ML pipeline) blocks on morsel — I cannot build cradle until there is a place to host inference primitives that don't drag candle into every binary.

five_whys_trace.q: Why is candle-as-dep unacceptable for cradle and friends?
five_whys_trace.a: Candle plus a 200MB model file forces the binary to ship as a tarball with assets. The wintermute ecosystem is built around single statically-linked binaries dropped into ~/.local/bin/. A 200MB asset breaks the install story.

five_whys_trace.q: Why does the install story matter so much that we're building a parallel ML primitives lib?
five_whys_trace.a: Because the wintermute tools that need ML decisions are all <50-neuron, <1MB-weight problems. Using candle for them is using a sledgehammer to crack a walnut — the cost of the dep dominates the cost of the decision.

five_whys_trace.q: Why ship inference primitives now, before the bake CLI or any real worked example?
five_whys_trace.a: Because the primitives are the contract everything else depends on. The bake CLI emits weights that target a specific primitive's API; the cat example composes those primitives; cradle imports them. Get the primitives right and stable first, then the rest can be built against a frozen API. This is PRD §7 Phase 0 literally.

ambiguities_resolved.question: The PRD describes a Cargo workspace with crates/morsel, crates/morsel-bake, crates/morsel-macros, plus examples/cat_meow. Autobuilder scaffolds a single crate. Which slice do we build first?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Scope v0.1 to PRD §7 Phase 0: the core `morsel` library crate (layer primitives). morsel-bake and the cat example are explicit non-goals for this iteration and will be follow-on PRDs.

ambiguities_resolved.question: PRD §9.3 asks 'no_std from day one or eventually?'. Day one if cheap. Is it cheap?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Defer to follow-on: f32::exp lives in libm-via-core but only with the libm crate as a dep. Adding a dep contradicts the max_deps=1 (proptest) constraint and adds a config dimension. Document as non-goal and revisit when a no_std consumer materializes.

ambiguities_resolved.question: PRD §4.5 promises 'allocation-free inference'. The autobuilder scaffold denies clippy::unwrap_used and forbids unsafe. Are caller-provided output buffers the right way to express this in safe Rust without unwraps?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Yes: every layer function takes a `&mut [f32]` output slice the caller owns. Shape mismatches are caught via debug_assert!. The hot path uses indexed slice arithmetic (clippy::indexing_slicing is `warn`, not `deny`, in the scaffold), no Vec, no Box, no Result-returning per-element ops.

ambiguities_resolved.question: AC7's 'allocation-free' is hard to prove via a Rust test. How do we make it falsifiable?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Two-pronged: (a) the API itself takes `&mut [f32]` outputs — there's nowhere to allocate inside the call; (b) a runtime test loops a composed pipeline 100 times against pre-allocated buffers and asserts it completes without panic. This is a structural guarantee plus a runtime smoke test; full allocation tracking would need a custom allocator (out of scope).

ambiguities_resolved.question: AC6 references an 'XOR-LSTM' worked example. The PRD's worked example is a cat audio classifier with real weights, which v0.1 cannot ship.
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Replace with a synthetic XOR-LSTM in the test suite: hand-set weights that classify a 4-symbol pattern. This exercises composition of every primitive (Lstm + Linear + Softmax + Argmax) without needing real training data, and is what PRD §7 Phase 0 explicitly proposes ('Hand-written weights.rs for a toy XOR-LSTM').
